pattern: Bucketing keys by use-frequency, with each bucket kept in recency order, lets you evict the least-frequently-used key (breaking ties by least-recently-used) and promote a key across buckets all in constant time.
hints: Eviction depends on two things at once: how often a key has been used, and among the least-used keys, which was touched longest ago.
Group keys by their current use count; the bucket holding the smallest count is where eviction happens, and within a bucket the oldest key loses.
Track the minimum frequency present; on every get or put bump the key into the next frequency bucket as a most-recent entry, and when full drop the oldest key from the minimum-frequency bucket.

edge_inputs.description: frequency tiebreak falls back to least-recently-used
capacity one keeps only the most recent key
a get raises a key's frequency and protects it from eviction
updating an existing key also counts as a use and changes its frequency
all keys reach equal frequency so eviction is purely recency-based

stress.description: long randomized get/put workload against a small capacity and small key space
stress.note: small key space forces frequent hits, evictions, and frequency ties

stress.description: put-heavy workload to stress eviction and tiebreaking paths
stress.note: heavy puts against a tiny capacity exercise the least-frequently-used eviction order
